children.name: Regulus
children.description: Kinglets and firecrests — Europe's and North America's smallest birds, weighing as little as 5 grams yet capable of surviving Scandinavian winters in spruce forest by constant foraging through the night. The goldcrest (kungsfågel — 'king bird') takes its royal name from the brilliant gold and orange crown stripe that is erected into a crest during display and aggressive encounters: the English 'goldcrest' and Latin Regulus ('little king') both reflect this crown. The goldcrest breeds in almost every Swedish spruce forest and migrates in vast numbers each autumn, moving in loose mixed flocks with tits through the coastal zone; in some years hundreds of thousands cross the Swedish coast on a single October day. The firecrest, with its additional white supercilium and blazing orange crown, is slowly expanding northward into southern Sweden as temperatures warm.

children.name: Regulus regulus
children.description: The kungsfågel — Europe's smallest bird at 5–6 g and one of Sweden's most abundant species, breeding in every spruce and fir forest throughout the country. Its thin, very high-pitched song — a series of rising and falling needle-like notes ending in a short flourish — is one of the most characteristic sounds of Swedish conifer forest. Survives Swedish winters by feeding continuously through daylight hours, searching for tiny invertebrate eggs and overwintering insects among needle clusters. Autumn migration concentrations along the Swedish coast can involve hundreds of thousands of individuals on a single day. The golden crown stripe is fluffed into a crest during male territorial displays.

children.name: Regulus ignicapilla
children.description: The brandkronad kungsfågel — slightly more colourful than the goldcrest with a bold white supercilium, black eye-stripe, and blazing orange-flame centre to the crown that gives the species its name. Historically a rarity in Sweden, breeding only occasionally in Skåne, but the species has been expanding its range northward over recent decades, establishing small breeding populations in southern Swedish mixed forests. Its song is similar to the goldcrest's but louder and without the terminal flourish. The bronze-coloured shoulder patch visible at close range is a unique feature among European passerines.

children.name: Regulus satrapa
children.description: North America's counterpart to the goldcrest — nearly identical in size and habits, inhabiting boreal and montane conifer forests from Alaska and Canada south through the Appalachians and Rockies. Both sexes show a brilliant golden crown stripe bordered in black; males add a flame-orange centre raised in display. Like the goldcrest it is a year-round resident in much of its range, surviving bitter northern winters through near-constant foraging. Its extremely thin, high-pitched song is at the limit of human hearing and among the highest-pitched songs of any North American bird. The species name satrapa ('satrap', a Persian governor) mirrors the 'little king' theme of the genus.

children.name: Regulus calendula
children.description: The most abundant kinglet in North America and slightly larger than the Golden-crowned, breeding in boreal forests from Alaska to Newfoundland and wintering across the southern United States and Mexico. The ruby crown — a vivid scarlet patch on the crown of males — is normally concealed under plain olive feathers and revealed only in moments of excitement or display, suddenly transforming a drab bird into something spectacular. Despite its tiny size it has an extraordinarily loud, complex song for its mass — a cascading series of high notes and chatter audible at considerable distance. It is noticeably more active and flick-winged than the Golden-crowned Kinglet.

children.name: Regulus teneriffae
children.description: A small kinglet endemic to the Canary Islands, distributed across Tenerife, La Palma, La Gomera, El Hierro and Gran Canaria in Macaronesian laurel forest and pine zones. Long treated as a subspecies of the Common Firecrest but recognised as a distinct species on the basis of its isolated range, distinct song, and subtle plumage differences — including a less bold supercilium and warmer-toned flanks. It inhabits dense laurisilva (ancient laurel cloud forest) and Canarian pine woodlands, foraging much like the firecrest by gleaning tiny arthropods from leaves and bark. Its restricted island habitats make it vulnerable to habitat change and invasive species.

children.name: Regulus madeirensis
children.description: Endemic to the island of Madeira, where it inhabits the ancient laurisilva cloud forest on the northern slopes and high-altitude heathland — one of the last remnants of the subtropical laurel forest that once covered much of southern Europe. Separated from the Common Firecrest by its isolated oceanic range and by minor but consistent differences in plumage and vocalisation. The Madeiran laurisilva is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and its largest remaining natural area; the firecrest is one of the island's characteristic birds, along with the Trocaz Pigeon. The total population is estimated in the low tens of thousands, confined to a single island.
